past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed 8 years ago
Major depressive disorder, single episode, 4 years ago, treated and resolved
Irritable bowel syndrome, diagnosed 6 years ago
Chronic low back pain after motor vehicle accident 3 years ago
Childhood history of sexual abuse disclosed during psychiatric evaluation

medications.indication: Chronic low back pain, muscle spasm

medications.frequency: as needed for acute anxiety, max twice daily

family_history: Maternal grandmother with epilepsy, well-controlled on medication
No family history of movement disorders or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband and two children (ages 5 and 7) in a single-story home
social_history.stressors: Ongoing contentious divorce proceedings, recently separated from husband 3 months ago, custody dispute over children
social_history.exercise: Previously active with yoga and running, stopped 2 months ago due to tremor and episodes

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent, normal rate and prosody. Affect mildly anxious but cooperative. Attention intact, able to spell WORLD backward. Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. Language intact, no paraphasic errors. Fund of knowledge appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 4mm to 2mm. Fundoscopy normal, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact. Intermittent convergence spasm noted during testing with convergent eye deviation and miosis, resolving spontaneously. No sustained nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact V1-V3 bilaterally, masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in upper extremities. Variable resistance to passive movement in bilateral lower extremities, not velocity-dependent, inconsistent with spasticity or rigidity. Strength testing: Upper extremities 5/5 throughout bilaterally. Right lower extremity: Hip flexion 4/5 with give-way pattern (initial full effort followed by sudden collapse), knee extension 4/5 with give-way weakness, ankle dorsiflexion 3/5 with give-way pattern. Left lower extremity: Hip flexion 4-/5 with give-way pattern, knee extension 4/5 with give-way weakness, ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Hoover sign positive bilaterally: involuntary hip extension noted when testing contralateral hip flexion, with significantly greater force generated involuntarily than during direct testing. No pronator drift in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in the entire right leg in a non-dermatomal distribution, with a sharp midline split extending to the trunk. Vibration sense reportedly absent in right leg but intact in left. However, tuning fork placed on the right side of the frontal bone of the skull reportedly not felt, which is physiologically inconsistent as bone conducts vibration bilaterally. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes when formally tested with distraction.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. Reflexes are normal and symmetric, inconsistent with the degree of reported weakness.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing on the left is intact. Right heel-to-shin shows variable performance with inconsistent errors not following a cerebellar pattern. Rapid alternating movements normal bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Walks with dramatic, lurching gait with excessive truncal sway but without falling. Demonstrates astasia-abasia pattern with marked swaying and flailing of arms but consistently catches herself. When asked to perform tandem gait, sways dramatically but does not fall. Gait improves significantly when distracted by cognitive tasks (serial subtraction). Romberg testing shows exaggerated sway with arms out, body swaying in multiple directions including forward and backward, inconsistent with true proprioceptive loss.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient experienced a paroxysmal episode lasting approximately 90 seconds: she became unresponsive, eyes closed tightly with visible flutter, bilateral asynchronous jerking movements of all four extremities with variable amplitude and frequency, side-to-side head movements, and pelvic thrusting. No cyanosis. Bilateral arms could be gently repositioned and showed resistance to eye opening. Episode terminated gradually. Patient was tearful and reported no memory of the event. Throughout the event, she was able to avoid hitting the bedrail when her arm was passively dropped toward it.

patient.chief_complaint: Episodes of whole-body shaking and unresponsiveness, intermittent tremor of the right hand, and progressive weakness in both legs
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Pooja Iyer is a 42-year-old right-handed elementary school teacher who presents for inpatient evaluation after six weeks of progressive neurological symptoms that have left her unable to work. Her symptoms began approximately six weeks ago with an intermittent coarse tremor of the right hand that would come and go throughout the day, lasting minutes to hours at a time. She describes the tremor as most prominent when she is stressed or being observed, and she notes it sometimes stops when she is absorbed in an activity like reading to her children, only to return when she becomes aware of it again. Two weeks after the tremor onset, she began experiencing episodes of unresponsiveness accompanied by whole-body shaking. These episodes have occurred approximately eight times, most recently yesterday. Witnesses, including her estranged husband and her mother, describe episodes lasting one to three minutes during which she becomes unresponsive, her eyes close tightly, and she develops asynchronous bilateral jerking movements of her arms and legs. The movements are described as variable in speed and amplitude, sometimes waxing and waning. Her husband reports that during one episode, she appeared to be crying while shaking. After the episodes, she typically becomes tearful and states she cannot remember what happened, but she does not appear confused and is able to speak clearly within seconds of the episode ending. She has never injured herself during these events, never bitten her tongue, and has never been incontinent. Over the past three weeks, she has also developed progressive weakness in both legs, worse on the right than the left. She reports difficulty walking and has been using a cane provided by her primary care physician. She states the weakness is constant but seems worse on days when she is more stressed. She was initially seen at an outside emergency department four weeks ago after her second shaking episode, where a CT head was normal. She was started on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily for presumed new-onset seizures. Despite the medication, her episodes have continued at the same frequency, which prompted referral to neurology. She was admitted two days ago for video-EEG monitoring. She reports significant life stressors including separation from her husband three months ago and an ongoing custody battle. She describes increased anxiety and difficulty sleeping since the separation. She had a prior episode of non-epileptic events at age 19 during college that resolved spontaneously, which she did not initially disclose.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes. No epileptiform discharges in drowsiness or sleep.

artifacts.description: Significant movement artifact during two captured clinical events, obscuring EEG but with readable underlying channels showing no epileptiform activity
Intermittent EMG artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity during interictal periods

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Normal symmetrical slowing as physiological response. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Patient reported feeling dizzy but no clinical event occurred.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring captured two habitual clinical events. Event 1 (day 1, 14:32): Patient alerted staff to an impending episode. She was observed on video to close her eyes tightly, followed by bilateral asynchronous limb jerking lasting 2 minutes 15 seconds with side-to-side head movement and pelvic thrusting. The EEG during this event showed a normal awake background rhythm with movement artifact superimposed. There was no electrographic seizure correlate, no post-ictal slowing, and no attenuation. Event 2 (day 2, 09:18): Similar semiology lasting 1 minute 40 seconds, triggered after a phone call. Again, no EEG correlate. Interictal EEG throughout 72 hours of monitoring is entirely normal with no epileptiform discharges. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Movement artifact during clinical events partially obscured some channels, but sufficient channels remained interpretable to confirm absence of ictal EEG pattern.

additional_observations: No mass lesion or abnormal enhancement
No white matter lesions to suggest demyelinating disease
Normal basal ganglia and thalami bilaterally
Normal cervical spinal cord signal on sagittal sequences through the foramen magnum
mri.impression: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast is entirely normal. No structural lesion, demyelinating disease, infarction, hemorrhage, or mass to explain the patient's symptoms. No findings to suggest multiple sclerosis, stroke, or space-occupying lesion. Normal study. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 132 mEq/L (L), Chloride 93 mEq/L (L).

background.sleep_features: Normal NREM sleep architecture. No epileptiform discharges in sleep.

artifacts.description: Movement artifact during captured events, underlying interpretable channels show normal background

output.impression: Extended video-EEG monitoring captured two additional habitual events on days 3 and 4 of monitoring. Both events demonstrated the same semiology: eyes tightly closed, bilateral asynchronous limb jerking with waxing and waning intensity, side-to-side head movements, and pelvic thrusting. Duration ranged from 1 to 3 minutes. During both events, the EEG showed a preserved normal background alpha rhythm with no ictal pattern, no post-ictal slowing, and no epileptiform discharges. A total of four habitual events have now been captured over 96 hours of monitoring, all without any EEG correlate. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: None significant. Adequate sampling with multiple events captured.

output.impression: MRI of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine is essentially normal. There is mild degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and L5-S1, which is age-appropriate and incidental, without neural compression or spinal canal stenosis. No myelopathy, cord compression, demyelinating lesions, or structural abnormality to explain the patient's lower extremity weakness or sensory complaints. The normal spinal imaging, in conjunction with normal brain MRI and normal EMG/NCS, does not support an organic neurological cause for the patient's symptoms. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: psychogenic non-epileptic seizures diagnosis management treatment

results.title: Diagnosis and Management of Psychogenic Nonepileptic Seizures
results.key_finding: Video-EEG is the gold standard for PNES diagnosis. CBT is the only evidence-based psychotherapy for PNES. Antiepileptic drugs should be tapered.

results.title: Functional Neurological Disorder: New Subtypes and Shared Mechanisms
results.key_finding: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (e.g., Hoover sign, tremor entrainment) rather than by exclusion. FND is a disorder of voluntary motor control, not malingering.

results.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of CBT vs Standard Medical Care for PNES (CODES trial)
results.key_finding: CBT for PNES significantly reduced seizure frequency at 12 months compared to standard medical care. Number needed to treat = 5.7.

output.summary: Current evidence supports video-EEG as the diagnostic gold standard for PNES. Positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness) are important for diagnosing functional weakness. The CODES trial demonstrated CBT as effective treatment for PNES. AEDs should be tapered as they are ineffective and carry risk of adverse effects. A compassionate, non-judgmental approach to diagnosis disclosure is critical for treatment engagement.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional limb weakness

differential.key_distinguishing: Family history of epilepsy in grandmother initially raises concern. However, habitual events captured on video-EEG show no ictal EEG correlate. Semiology is atypical for epilepsy: eyes tightly closed, asynchronous movements, variable amplitude and frequency, pelvic thrusting, waxing and waning pattern, and no post-ictal confusion. Normal post-event prolactin. Events not controlled by therapeutic levetiracetam level.

Young woman with progressive neurological symptoms could raise concern for MS. However, brain MRI is entirely normal with no white matter lesions. Spine MRI shows no demyelination. No clinical dissemination in time and space. Normal inflammatory markers. Weakness pattern with give-way quality and positive Hoover sign is inconsistent with upper motor neuron lesion.

Weakness could suggest neuromuscular junction disorder. However, weakness does not demonstrate fatigable pattern, no ptosis, no diplopia, no bulbar symptoms. EMG/NCS normal with no decremental response. Weakness pattern shows give-way quality inconsistent with true neuromuscular weakness.

Subacute onset over weeks rather than acute onset. No vascular territory distribution. Normal brain MRI with no DWI restriction. Age and risk factor profile make stroke very unlikely. Examination findings inconsistent with upper motor neuron pattern.

Episodes of altered awareness with movement abnormalities could suggest autoimmune encephalitis. However, no progressive cognitive decline, no psychiatric symptoms beyond pre-existing conditions, normal brain MRI, normal EEG background, and negative neuronal antibodies argue strongly against this diagnosis.

optimal_actions.action: Admit for continuous video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical events with no EEG correlate, confirming psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, sensory midline split)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split, distractible gait abnormality

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All normal, including normal post-event prolactin supporting non-epileptic event

optimal_actions.action: Recognize positive functional signs on examination as diagnostic of FND, not merely excluding other diagnoses
Communicate diagnosis to patient using clear, empathetic, non-judgmental approach, explaining that the symptoms are real but caused by a problem with how the nervous system is functioning
Taper and discontinue levetiracetam as it is not indicated for PNES
Refer for psychiatric evaluation to address comorbid PTSD, anxiety, and depression
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification of PTSD, depression, and dissociative features

optimal_actions.action: Refer for specialized cognitive behavioral therapy for PNES and functional neurological symptoms
Arrange outpatient multidisciplinary follow-up with neurology, psychiatry, and physiotherapy

critical_actions: Capture habitual events on video-EEG and confirm absence of ictal EEG correlate
Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, give-way weakness) rather than diagnosing by exclusion alone
Communicate the diagnosis of FND/PNES to the patient in an empathetic, non-dismissive manner, validating that symptoms are real
Taper and discontinue antiepileptic medication that is not indicated
Refer for psychiatric evaluation and evidence-based psychotherapy (CBT for PNES)
Do not dismiss the patient's symptoms as 'faking' or malingering
contraindicated_actions: Continuing or escalating antiepileptic drug therapy when events are confirmed as non-epileptic
Telling the patient their symptoms are imaginary, fake, or 'all in their head'
Ordering excessive additional investigations after diagnosis is established, perpetuating diagnostic uncertainty
Prescribing benzodiazepines to abort non-epileptic events, which reinforces the sick role and risks dependence
Discharging without a clear follow-up plan including psychotherapy referral
Performing lumbar puncture when there is no clinical indication
Attributing symptoms to malingering without evidence of deliberate fabrication
key_reasoning_points: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, distractibility of symptoms) rather than solely by exclusion of organic disease
Video-EEG capturing habitual events without ictal EEG correlate is the gold standard for diagnosing PNES, and this case had multiple events captured

The semiology of PNES differs from epileptic seizures: eyes closed (rather than open), asynchronous movements, variable amplitude and frequency, waxing and waning pattern, pelvic thrusting, and lack of post-ictal confusion
Normal post-event prolactin supports (but does not definitively prove) a non-epileptic mechanism, as prolactin is typically elevated after generalized tonic-clonic seizures but not after PNES
Psychiatric comorbidity (PTSD, depression, anxiety, dissociative features) is present in the majority of patients with FND/PNES and must be addressed as part of treatment
The family history of epilepsy in the grandmother is a potential red herring that might lead to anchoring bias toward an epilepsy diagnosis
The overlap between features of this case and epilepsy (family history, paroxysmal events, failure to respond to AEDs) makes this a moderate-difficulty case requiring careful clinical reasoning
CBT is the only evidence-based psychotherapy for PNES, as demonstrated by the CODES trial, and should be a cornerstone of treatment

clinical_pearls: Hoover sign is one of the most reliable positive signs for functional leg weakness: the patient generates involuntary hip extension when asked to flex the contralateral hip, demonstrating greater force than during direct testing
Eyes closed during a paroxysmal event is one of the most specific signs of PNES, as patients with epileptic seizures typically have their eyes open
The diagnosis of FND should be framed positively to patients: 'Your nervous system is not damaged, but it is not functioning correctly' rather than 'We cannot find anything wrong'
Up to 10-20% of patients referred to epilepsy monitoring units are found to have PNES rather than epileptic seizures
Approximately 50-80% of patients with PNES have comorbid PTSD, depression, or anxiety, making psychiatric evaluation essential
